p.strong: Trace of parse_expr(0) on a + b * c

div.p: When parse_expr(0) encounters + (left_bp=4), it calls parse_expr(5) for the right side. Inside that call, * (left_bp=6) satisfies 6 ≥ 5 and captures b and c before returning. A second + would have left_bp=4 < 5 and would not enter the loop — it would be left for the outer call to handle.
The right_bp = left_bp + 1 asymmetry is what creates left-associativity. For right-associative operators, use right_bp = left_bp (same value).

g.text: STEP 7 — Back in parse_expr(5): build Mul node
STEP 8 — Back in parse_expr(0): build Add node

div.p: Call parse_prefix() → consume token a Return IDENT("a") as left
Peek next token: + left_bp(+) = 4 ≥ min_bp=0 → enter loop Consume +

New invocation to parse the right-hand side of + This recursive call has min_bp=5, meaning only operators with left_bp ≥ 5 can capture operands. + has left_bp=4 < 5, so it cannot steal from this call. * has left_bp=6 ≥ 5, so it can.
Call parse_prefix() → consume token b Return IDENT("b") as left
Peek next token: * left_bp(*) = 6 ≥ min_bp=5 → enter loop Consume *
Call parse_prefix() → consume token c Peek next token: EOF (or ;) No operator with left_bp ≥ 7 → exit loop immediately Return IDENT("c")
right = IDENT("c") (from step 6) Build Mul(left="b", right="c") Peek next token: EOF — no operator ≥ 5 Exit loop, return Mul(b, c)
right = Mul(b, c) (from step 7) Build Add(left="a", right=Mul(b,c)) Peek next token: EOF — no operator ≥ 0 Exit loop, return final AST
